title: The True Cost of Yacht Ownership
excerpt: The purchase price is only the beginning. Here is an honest breakdown of dockage, insurance, fuel, maintenance, and crew, plus the rule of thumb that keeps owners from being surprised.
author: Clark Haley
keywords: cost of yacht ownership, yacht ownership costs, how much does it cost to own a yacht, boat dockage cost, yacht insurance Florida, yacht maintenance budget, 10 percent rule yacht, Haley Yachts buyer guide
heroAlt: A yacht moored at a Florida marina at sunset
bodyHtml:
The happiest boat owners I know are the ones who walked in with their eyes open on cost. The unhappy ones almost always got surprised, not by the purchase price, which they planned for, but by what it costs to run the boat after they own it. So let me give you the honest version. Budgeting for ownership before you buy, not after, is the single best thing you can do to enjoy the boat instead of resenting it.

The five real costs of ownership

Beyond the price of the boat, ownership comes down to five recurring categories. The size of each depends on your boat, your location, and how you use her, but they all show up.

Dockage. Where you keep the boat is often the biggest fixed cost. In South Florida, a slip at a good marina is priced by the foot and varies widely between a working marina and a premium facility in Palm Beach or Jupiter. You are paying for location, protection, and amenities. A mooring or dry storage is cheaper for smaller boats, but for most yachts a wet slip is the reality, and seasonal demand down here keeps those numbers firm.

Insurance. Florida is a named-storm state, and that shapes your premium. Insurers care about the boat's age, value, condition, your experience, and your hurricane plan. A clean recent survey and agreed-value coverage work in your favor. Skimping here is a mistake, because the one season you need it is the season that pays for years of premiums.

Fuel. This one scales hard with how you run the boat and what is in the engine room. A pair of diesels at cruise burns real money on a Bahamas crossing, and a fast express boat burns more when you push her. Fuel is the most variable line on the list. Run the boat a lot and it dominates. Keep her mostly at the dock and it barely registers.

"The purchase price gets all the attention. The running costs are what actually determine whether you love owning the boat."Clark Haley, Haley Yachts

Maintenance. Boats live in a harsh environment, and saltwater is relentless. Plan for routine service on engines and generators, bottom paint and haul-outs, zincs, canvas, electronics, and the steady drip of small items that come with any complex machine. Deferred maintenance is the most expensive kind, because small problems on a boat rarely stay small. The owners who budget for upkeep keep their boats worth more at resale, too.

Crew, if applicable. Smaller boats a couple can run themselves carry no crew cost, which is a big part of their appeal. Once you move up in size, you may want a captain for some trips or a full-time captain and mate. If crew is in your picture, it is usually the largest single line after the boat itself, so factor it in honestly before you size up.

The 10 percent rule of thumb

Here is the shorthand the industry uses, and it holds up well enough to plan around: budget roughly 10 percent of the boat's value per year for the total cost of ownership, covering dockage, insurance, fuel, maintenance, and the rest. On a boat worth one million dollars, that is on the order of one hundred thousand dollars a year.

Is it exact? No. A boat you keep at a modest marina and run lightly can come in well under. A boat you run hard, keep at a premium slip, and crew can run over. But as a planning figure to make sure you are not blindsided, 10 percent is the number I give buyers. If that figure changes how you feel about a particular boat, far better to learn it now than a year in.

Budget before you buy, not after

The mistake is treating the purchase price as the cost. It is the entry fee. The smart move is to build the full annual picture before you make an offer, decide what level of running cost fits your life, and let that inform which boat you actually buy. Sometimes the right answer is a slightly smaller boat you run without crew and enjoy freely, rather than a larger one that becomes a second job and a second budget.

A good broker will give you this honestly rather than gloss over it to close a sale. The buyers who hear the real numbers up front are the ones still smiling three seasons later.

If you want a realistic ownership budget for a specific boat you are considering, I am glad to build it with you. Reach out to Clark Haley at Haley Yachts and we will run the real numbers together before you commit, so you buy the boat that fits your life and not just your driveway. That conversation is a normal part of how we work with buyers.
